_metadata.description: Other/inactive ingredients database for supplement excipients and additives

other_ingredients.notes: Natural soluble fiber from acacia trees. Prebiotic properties and excellent safety profile.
Natural acid (vinegar component) used for pH control and preservation.

Natural thickening and gelling agent derived from red algae. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) and commonly used as a vegan alternative to gelatin. May provide beneficial dietary fiber. Excellent clean label choice.
Used as extraction solvent or preservative. Generally evaporates during processing. GRAS approved.
Rare natural sugar with 70% sweetness of sucrose but minimal calories. Does not affect blood sugar. FDA recognized as GRAS.
Prebiotic oligosaccharide carbohydrate used for gut-support formulations; mapped as non-scorable identity pending dedicated efficacy scoring policy.
Natural yellow/orange colorant from annatto seeds. GRAS status. Clean label color.
Natural purple/red pigment from berries and fruits. Antioxidant properties. Clean label color. GRAS approved.
Apple cider vinegar used in gummies for digestive health claims. Contains acetic acid. GRAS approved.
Concentrated apple puree used for flavor and natural sweetness. Clean label ingredient. GRAS approved.
Natural plant-derived gum from Acacia trees. Excellent clean label binder and stabilizer. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS).
Natural red/pink coloring from beetroot juice. Rich in betalains, provides antioxidant benefits. GRAS approved.
common_uses: clean label alternative to artificial colors

other_ingredients.notes: Various berry purees used for flavor and color. Clean label ingredients. GRAS approved.
Natural purple/red colorant from black carrot juice, rich in anthocyanins. Popular in gummies as a clean label alternative to artificial colors. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from black cherry extracts and compounds. Provides authentic black cherry taste to supplement formulations.
Black pepper extract containing piperine. Enhances absorption of other nutrients. GRAS approved.
Nutrient-rich byproduct of sugar refining. Contains minerals. GRAS approved.
Brown seaweed traditionally used for thyroid support due to iodine content. GRAS approved.
Natural blue/purple colorant from blueberry juice. Rich in anthocyanins. Clean label. GRAS approved.
pH adjuster used in small amounts. Common in food processing.
Natural calcium salt that's easily absorbed. Often preferred over calcium carbonate for bioavailability. Generally well-tolerated.
FDA GRAS-approved anticaking agent, emulsifier, and lubricant. Listed in FDA regulations 21 CFR 172.863 and 175.300. CAS Registry Number: 542-42-7.
Non-psychoactive cannabinoid from hemp. Precursor cannabinoid. Regulatory status similar to CBD.
Mildly psychoactive cannabinoid from hemp. Often marketed for sleep. Regulatory status varies.
Natural emulsifier from canola/rapeseed. Alternative to soy lecithin. GRAS approved.
Natural orange colorant from carrot juice. Rich in beta-carotene. Clean label. GRAS approved.
Natural plant fiber used as a bulking agent and source of dietary fiber. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Excellent clean label choice for adding fiber content.
Calming herb extract used for relaxation and sleep support. GRAS approved.
Combination of natural cherry extracts and artificial cherry compounds to provide cherry taste. Natural extracts from real cherries combined with synthetic compounds that replicate cherry flavor.
Chicory-derived prebiotic fiber used for sweetness reduction and gut health support.
Chili pepper containing capsaicin. Used for metabolism and circulation. GRAS approved.
Water-soluble form of chlorophyll used as green colorant. GRAS approved.

Natural preservative and pH adjuster. While naturally found in citrus, commercial form is typically derived from fermented sources. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Clean label preservative option.
Natural fiber from citrus fruits used as bulking agent and prebiotic. Clean label alternative to synthetic fibers.
Natural fat extracted from cocoa beans. Used as a carrier for fat-soluble ingredients and to improve texture in supplements. Melts at body temperature.
Various forms of coconut-derived ingredients used as carriers or fillers for fat-soluble ingredients. Generally safe, well-tolerated, and offer clean label appeal.
Dried coconut in granulated form. Used for flavor and texture. GRAS approved.
Broken down collagen protein for better absorption. Popular for skin, hair, nail, and joint health. GRAS approved.
Cranberry concentrate used for urinary tract health. Clean label ingredient. GRAS approved.
Purified water with minerals removed. Standard pharmaceutical-grade water.
Proprietary blend of natural sweeteners typically including stevia, monk fruit, and other natural sources. Clean label alternative to artificial sweeteners.

aliases: Echinacea purpurea (aerial and root parts) 4:1 extract
other_ingredients.notes: Immune-supporting herb extract. Popular for cold and flu support. GRAS approved.
Elderberry extract used for immune support. Popular cold and flu remedy. GRAS approved.
Natural colorant from elderberry juice. Rich in anthocyanins. Clean label ingredient. GRAS approved.
Cellulose derivative used as coating agent and controlled release matrix. Provides moisture barrier and taste masking. GRAS approved with excellent safety profile.
Natural fruit and vegetable juices used for flavor, color, and sweetness. Clean label ingredient.
Dehydrated and powdered plants used for natural color and flavor. Generally safe and may provide some antioxidant benefits. Premium clean-label alternative to artificial dyes.

aliases: containing one or more of the following:
other_ingredients.notes: Generic coloring term. May indicate natural or artificial colors - specific identification recommended. Generally safe but vague labeling.
Ginger root extract used for digestive and anti-inflammatory benefits. GRAS approved.
Natural ginger flavoring. GRAS approved.
Natural sugar derived from corn or wheat starch. Provides quick energy and acts as a binding agent. GRAS approved by FDA.
Natural humectant derived from vegetable oils. Essential for gummy texture and shelf life. GRAS approved.
Emulsifier derived from glycerin and fatty acids. Generally recognized as safe.

Natural sweetener derived from licorice root (Glycyrrhiza glabra). Approximately 30-50x sweeter than sugar. Used in small amounts as flavor enhancer. MAG form commonly used in supplements. May raise blood pressure in sensitive individuals with excessive use.
Natural colorant from grape juice. Rich in anthocyanins. Clean label alternative to artificial colors. GRAS approved.

aliases: Full Spectrum Hemp Aerial Parts Extract
organic broad-spectrum hemp aerial part extract
other_ingredients.notes: Hemp extract containing various phytocannabinoids. Legal hemp contains <0.3% THC. Regulatory status varies by state.
Coconut oil that has been hydrogenated for stability. Used as carrier and for texture. Less clean label than unhydrogenated.
Prebiotic fiber commonly derived from chicory root. Supports gut health and texture in gummies.
Red algae used for mineral content and as a thickening agent. Popular in wellness products. GRAS approved.
Prebiotic fiber syrup used for texture and mild sweetness. Generally well-tolerated and used as a fiber source.
Natural fruit juice concentrates used for color and flavor. Clean label ingredients. GRAS approved.
Protein for hair, skin, and nail health. Derived from wool or feathers. GRAS approved.
Sugar alcohol derived from lactose. Lower glycemic impact than sugar, commonly used in diabetic foods. GRAS approved.
Natural emulsifier derived from sunflowers. Often preferred over soy lecithin due to non-GMO and allergen concerns. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Premium clean label emulsifier.
Calming herb extract used for relaxation and sleep support. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from lemon extracts and compounds. Provides authentic lemon taste to supplement formulations.

Widely used tablet and capsule lubricant. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Primary considerations are source (plant vs animal) and vegan/halal/kosher suitability. No strong evidence of reduced nutrient absorption at typical use levels.
Inorganic compound used as antacid and pharmaceutical excipient. FDA-listed inactive ingredient for oral tablets. Acts as adsorbent and anti-caking agent. GRAS for food contact use.
Natural fruit acid found in apples and other fruits. Used for tartness and as a natural preservative. Well-tolerated and beneficial for energy metabolism.
Natural disaccharide sugar derived from malted grains. Less sweet than sucrose, commonly used in food products. GRAS approved.
Natural sweetener from malted grains. Less refined than corn syrup. Used in gummies for sweetness and texture. GRAS approved.
Mango puree used for flavor and natural sweetness. Clean label ingredient. GRAS approved.
Natural sweeteners derived from maple sap or sugar cane. Contain minerals and trace nutrients.
Plant-derived cellulose used as bulking agent and anticaking agent. Insoluble fiber. GRAS approved.

aliases: organic Aronia Berry (Sorbaronia mitschurinii) juice concentrate
other_ingredients.notes: Various fruit and vegetable juice concentrates used for natural color and flavor. Clean label ingredients. GRAS approved.
Various natural flavoring agents. GRAS approved.
Chemically or physically modified starch used as thickener and stabilizer. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Common in food and supplements.
Natural, calorie-free sweetener derived from a small melon. Its sweetness comes from antioxidants called mogrosides. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Excellent clean label choice.

aliases: mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids
other_ingredients.notes: Common emulsifiers derived from natural fats and oils. Help blend oil and water-based ingredients. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS).

aliases: Lion's Mane Mushroom Fruiting Body Extract
other_ingredients.notes: Functional mushroom extracts used for various health benefits. Popular for cognitive and immune support. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from apple sources. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from berry extracts and essential oils. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from blueberry sources. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from cinnamon bark. GRAS approved.
Natural coloring agents derived from plant, mineral, or other natural sources. Provides color without synthetic dyes.

aliases: fruit and vegetable juice color concentrates
other_ingredients.notes: Natural colorants derived from plants, fruits, and vegetables. Premium clean label alternative to synthetic dyes with additional antioxidant benefits.
Generic term for natural colorants derived from plant, mineral, or animal sources. Generally safe and preferred over artificial dyes. Includes various fruit and vegetable extracts used for coloring.
common_uses: clean label alternative to artificial colors

aliases: organic tropical flavor and other natural flavors
natural fruit flavors with other natural flavors
other_ingredients.notes: Flavoring substances derived from plant or animal sources. While generally recognized as safe, the term can encompass hundreds of compounds. Must be derived from natural sources per FDA regulations. Good clean label choice.
Natural flavoring agents derived from plant or animal sources. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from grape sources. GRAS approved.
Natural gums used as thickeners, binders, and stabilizers. Generally safe and can be a source of dietary fiber. May cause bloating or gas in some individuals. Good clean label choice.
Natural flavoring derived from mint essential oils. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from orange sources. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from citrus sources including orange and tangerine. FEMA GRAS approved as citrus-derived natural flavor complex. FDA 21 CFR Part 172 compliant.
Antioxidants used to prevent spoilage, such as fat-soluble Vitamin C (ascorbyl palmitate) or Vitamin E (tocopherols). Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) and beneficial. Premium clean label choice.

Premium natural flavoring from vanilla beans. Includes both natural vanillin compounds and full vanilla extracts. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Highly desirable clean label ingredient with excellent consumer perception.
Natural flavoring derived from watermelon sources. GRAS approved.
Plant-based materials used for natural coatings and glazing. Provides protective barrier and improves appearance. Vegan-friendly alternative to animal-derived waxes. Excellent clean label choice.
Natural sweetener derived from citrus bioflavonoids. Approximately 1500x sweeter than sugar. Used in very small amounts. E959 in EU. Derived from naringin in citrus peel.
Naturally occurring monounsaturated omega-9 fatty acid. Used as emulsifier and processing aid. Heart-healthy fat. GRAS approved.
Crystallized orange flavoring, typically made from concentrated natural orange extracts or oils. Used for flavor enhancement in lozenges and chewables.

Natural flavoring derived from orange extracts and compounds, including blood orange varieties. Provides authentic orange taste to supplement formulations. FEMA GRAS approved as citrus-derived natural flavor.
Organic sweetener from sugar cane. Less processed than refined sugar, retains some minerals.
Organic form of evaporated cane juice. Less processed than refined sugar, retains some minerals. Clean label natural sweetener option.
Soluble prebiotic fiber derived from guar gum. Often abbreviated PHGG.
Calming herb extract used for relaxation, anxiety, and sleep support. GRAS approved.
Natural flavoring derived from peach extracts and compounds. Provides authentic peach taste to supplement formulations.
Natural fiber from fruits, excellent for gelling and thickening. Provides additional health benefits including cholesterol support.
Acidifying agent used for pH control. Common in soft drinks. FDA approved.
Natural flavoring derived from pineapple extracts and compounds. Provides authentic pineapple taste to supplement formulations.
Pomegranate juice used for color, flavor, and antioxidant benefits. Clean label. GRAS approved.
Natural potassium salt with good bioavailability. Often used in electrolyte formulations and as a natural preservative.
Natural purple colorant from various berries, rich in anthocyanins. Clean label alternative to artificial colors. GRAS approved.
Natural purple colorant from purple carrot juice, rich in anthocyanins. Clean label natural color alternative. GRAS approved.
Natural saponin extract used as emulsifier and foaming agent. GRAS approved.
Natural raspberry fruit powder providing flavor and nutrients. Clean label ingredient from real fruit.
Natural, hypoallergenic ingredient used as a flow agent or lubricant. Serving as a clean-label alternative to magnesium stearate. Excellent premium clean label choice.
Natural rice-derived extract used as anticaking and flow agent. Clean label alternative to synthetic flow agents.
Extract from various seaweeds. Source of minerals and iodine. GRAS approved.
Natural silica source from bamboo, preferred over synthetic silicon dioxide. Provides natural flow properties with additional bioactive compounds.
Dried milk with fat removed. Contains milk allergen. Used as protein source and filler in some supplements.
Acidifying agent used for pH control. Common in food applications.
Natural pH regulator and buffer commonly used in food and supplements. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) with a long history of use. Clean label choice for pH adjustment.
Alkalizing agent and pH regulator. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) when used as a food additive. Common processing aid.

FDA GRAS-approved buffer and acidity regulator widely used safely in foods and supplements. Helps stabilize pH and acts as natural preservative. Consolidates two previous duplicate entries (Sodium Citrate and Trisodium Citrate - they are the same compound). Only consideration is moderate sodium content for sodium-restricted diets.
Sodium salt of lactic acid used as humectant and preservative. Helps maintain moisture in gummies. GRAS approved.
Acidity regulator and buffer used for pH control. Generally recognized as safe.
Double salt used as acidity regulator and leavening agent. GRAS approved.
Clean-label tablet lubricant and disintegrant. Used as alternative to magnesium stearate. Generally recognized as safe.
Fermentable soluble fiber used as a bulking agent and prebiotic.
Sugar alcohol used as sweetener and humectant. Generally safe but may cause digestive issues in large amounts.
Natural flavoring derived from spearmint extracts and compounds. Provides authentic spearmint taste to supplement formulations.
Chemically modified starch for improved stability and texture. FDA approved food additive.
Plant oil high in GLA (gamma-linolenic acid). Used for skin and hormonal health. GRAS approved.
Fatty acid used as excipient and lubricant. GRAS with long history of safe use. Source (plant vs animal) affects vegan suitability.

Natural, calorie-free sweetener from the Stevia rebaudiana plant. FDA GRAS status applies to HIGH-PURITY extracts (≥95% steviol glycosides) including rebaudioside A and stevioside. Whole stevia leaves and crude extracts are NOT FDA GRAS approved. Includes branded forms like BetterStevia, Truvia, and PureVia. Clean label alternative to artificial sweeteners.
Strawberry puree used for flavor and color. Clean label ingredient. GRAS approved.
Unrefined whole cane sugar retaining natural molasses and minerals. Less processed than white sugar. GRAS approved.
Emulsifier derived from sucrose and fatty acids. Used in gummies for texture. GRAS approved.
Natural sugars used for sweetening and palatability. Generally recognized as safe but should be consumed in moderation.
Branded partially hydrolyzed guar gum (PHGG) prebiotic fiber.
Sunfiber AG variant of PHGG prebiotic fiber.
Natural oil from sunflower seeds used as carrier for fat-soluble vitamins. Clean label ingredient. GRAS approved.
Soluble fiber derived from tapioca used for texture and sugar reduction in gummies.
Aromatic compounds from plants. Used for flavor and entourage effect in hemp products. GRAS approved.
Natural disaccharide sugar found in plants, fungi, and insects. Less sweet than sucrose, used for stability and moisture retention. GRAS approved.
Buffering agent and acidity regulator. Same as sodium citrate. GRAS approved.
Natural colorants derived from vegetables and fruits. Clean label alternative to artificial colors. GRAS approved.
Plant-derived emulsifiers. Used for texture and stability. GRAS approved.
Plant-derived humectant and solvent. Used to retain moisture and as a carrier for flavors. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Ensure proper sourcing to avoid contamination.
Plant-derived fatty acids and stearates used as natural lubricants and flow agents in manufacturing. Cleaner alternative to mineral-derived stearates. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS).
Branded bioactive collagen peptides clinically studied for skin health. Premium collagen ingredient.
Natural source of vitamin C from acerola cherry. Very high vitamin C content. Clean label ingredient.
Whole grain rice used as a natural filler and binder. Gluten-free. GRAS approved.
Common stabilizer and thickener. Generally safe; may cause digestive discomfort in some and may trigger sensitivity in individuals reactive to corn substrates depending on production.
Prebiotic oligosaccharide that feeds beneficial gut bacteria. Provides mild sweetness while supporting digestive health. GRAS approved.
Dried yogurt used for flavor and texture. Contains milk allergen. May provide some probiotic benefits if cultures are preserved.
Undecylenic acid derivative mapped for identity coverage; non-scorable until specific IQM quality forms are defined.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Glandular tissue ingredient. Identity is mapped for traceability; not scored in IQM without dedicated glandular quality criteria.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Natural yellow/orange colorant from annatto seeds. GRAS status. Widely used natural color.
Vitamin C used as natural antioxidant/preservative. GRAS status. Dual function: nutrient and preservative.
Natural red/pink colorant from beetroot. GRAS status. Clean label natural color.
Natural dietary fiber from sugar beet pulp. Used as natural filler and bulking agent. Clean-label ingredient with some prebiotic properties.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Mineral salt used in specialty products; mapped as non-scorable identity.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Salt of undecylenic acid used in niche formulations; kept as mapped non-scorable identity pending dedicated quality rubric.
Generic capsule delivery format descriptor. Not a specific ingredient but a formulation type. Classified as non-active to prevent unmapped inflation.
Cellulose derivative used as thickener and stabilizer. GRAS status with long history of safe use.
Cocoa powder used as flavoring and coloring in supplement formulations. Alkalized (Dutch process) cocoa has reduced polyphenol content. Contains trace theobromine. Benign flavoring agent.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Natural thickening agent derived from guar beans. GRAS status. Commonly used in supplements as a binder and thickener.

aliases: high and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose
High and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methyl-Cellulose
high and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methyl-Cellulose
high and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methyl Cellulose
High and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methyl-Cellulose and Calcium Citrate Laurate
High and low viscosity hydroxypropyl methylcellulose and Magnesium CitrateLaurate
High and low viscosity hydroxypropyl methyl-cellulose and CalciumCitrateLaurate
High and low viscosity hydroxypropyl methylcellulose and MagnesiumCitrateLaurate
High and low viscosity hydroxypropyl methyl-cellulose and MagnesiumCitrateLaurate
high and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methyl Cellulose and Calcium Citrate Laurate
high and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose and Magnesium Citrate Laurate
high and low viscosity Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose and CalciumCitrateLaurate
purified Water and Glycerin (vegetable source) gelcap
other_ingredients.notes: Composite vegetarian capsule shell made from HPMC (hydroxypropyl methylcellulose) with flow agents like magnesium/calcium citrate laurate. Common in Thorne and other premium supplement brands. Clean-label capsule material.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Label-level glandular descriptor mapped for identity coverage.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Natural flavoring derived from various berries. GRAS status. Popular blended fruit flavor.
Natural flavoring derived from mint. GRAS status. Common cooling flavor.
Natural flavoring derived from strawberries. GRAS status. Popular fruit flavor.
Natural flavoring derived from vanilla beans. GRAS status. Popular natural flavor in supplements.
Natural red/orange colorant from paprika peppers. GRAS status. Clean label natural color.
Branded bacteriophage prebiotic blend; mapped for identity and non-scorable classification pending dedicated rubric.

Natural polysaccharide produced by fermentation of tapioca starch by Aureobasidium pullulans. Used as vegan capsule shell material (Plantcaps). Clean-label, non-GMO alternative to HPMC and gelatin capsules.
Natural plant fiber from rice hulls or bran. Used as filler and flow agent in supplement manufacturing. Clean-label, allergen-free alternative to synthetic fillers.
Outer shell of rice grain. Used as natural filler and source of silica in supplement manufacturing. Natural and inert.
Branded algae phytonutrient complex; mapped for identity while clinical scoring form is curated.
Generic spice flavoring. Not a specific ingredient. Classified as non-active flavoring agent.
Natural blue/green colorant from spirulina algae. GRAS status. Clean label natural color.
Glandular tissue ingredient mapped for identity only.
Glandular tissue ingredient mapped for identity only.
Vitamin E used as natural antioxidant/preservative. GRAS status. Prevents oxidation in supplements.
Added from unmapped remediation for identity coverage. Treated as non-scorable pending dedicated scoring policy.
Natural yellow colorant from turmeric. GRAS status. Clean label natural color with antioxidant properties.
Pre-mixed vitamin blend used as base ingredient in supplement manufacturing. Not a specific active ingredient - individual vitamins should be listed separately.
Generic whole food base or fruit/vegetable concentrate blend used as nutritional carrier in supplement formulations. Not a specific active ingredient.
A processing aid used for filtering and purification of ingredients. Not typically present in the final product as it's removed during processing. Generally considered safe.
Branded form of colloidal silicon dioxide used as anti-caking agent and glidant. Generally recognized as safe.
Processing/pH-adjustment excipient entry for ingredient normalization and mapping. Not scored as therapeutic active.
Natural or artificial flavoring used to provide apple taste. Generally considered safe for flavoring purposes.
A specific type of coating using water-based systems, often listed as a sub-ingredient. Provides protective barrier and improved swallowing. Generally considered safe.
A natural wax produced by honey bees. Used as a coating or glazing agent for tablets and softgels to improve appearance and swallowing. Considered safe.

Plant pollen collected by bees. Often used as an active ingredient in energy and immunity supplements. WARNING: HIGH RISK allergen - can cause severe reactions in individuals with pollen or bee allergies. Classified as HIGH severity allergen.
Clay/mineral excipient used as processing aid or binder in some products.
Brand/trademark complex labels that are not chemically specific ingredients by themselves.
While an active ingredient for calcium, it is also frequently used passively as an inert filler or opacifier (to make capsules opaque) due to its low cost.
Mineral salt used in processing/formulation context; treated as non-scorable excipient mapping row.
Blend of calcium forms for bone health support.
Modified cellulose used as thickener, binder, and stabilizer. FDA approved food additive with GRAS status. Derived from plant cellulose.
Carob-derived ingredient naming variant commonly used for flavor/carrier context.
An enzyme that breaks down hydrogen peroxide. Generally considered safe and beneficial for cellular protection.
General or specific terms for coatings, which are passive ingredients that protect the tablet or capsule, mask taste, or aid in swallowing. Composition varies but generally considered safe.
First milk produced by mammals after giving birth, rich in antibodies and growth factors. Used for immune support. Note: May be considered an active ingredient in some contexts.
FDA labeling requirement indicating that subsequent ingredients comprise less than 2% of the product by weight. Not an actual ingredient but a regulatory disclosure statement.
A common plant-derived starch used as a binder, filler, or disintegrant. Generally safe, but non-GMO/organic sources are often preferred.
Modified cellulose used as tablet disintegrant to aid dissolution. FDA approved excipient with excellent safety profile. Derived from plant cellulose.
Cyclic oligosaccharide used to improve solubility and stability of active ingredients. FDA approved GRAS ingredient. Enhances bioavailability of poorly soluble compounds.
Dairy-based carrier/vehicle terms used for composition context; not scored as actives.
Polyglycerol-based emulsifier for dispersion and texture stability.
Carbohydrate polymer derived from starch used as a binding agent and bulking agent. Source plant not specified - could be corn, potato, rice, or other starch sources.
An enzyme that breaks down starch into sugars. Generally considered safe and beneficial for digestion.
Used as a tablet binder and filler. While it provides calcium and phosphorus, its primary role in 'other ingredients' is often mechanical. Generally considered safe.
Branded form of dicalcium phosphate dihydrate used as a direct compression filler-binder in tablets. Generally recognized as safe.
Branded delivery-system label term used as a carrier/technology descriptor. Treated as recognized non-scorable identity in scoring.
Buffering/phosphate salt used in formulation and stability systems.
Spray-dried canola oil used as a carrier or coating agent in supplements. Generally recognized as safe.
A specialized coating designed to prevent the tablet from dissolving in the stomach, allowing it to pass into the intestines. Key passive ingredient for targeted delivery. Generally considered safe.
Generic essential-oil descriptor row without botanical identity. Kept as recognized non-scorable until a specific oil is declared.

A high-quality, healthy fat often used as a neutral carrier for fat-soluble vitamins (like D, E, K, A) and other lipophilic active ingredients in liquid or softgel formulations. Primarily serves as a passive base.
Fatty acid profile subcomponents used as compositional descriptors; mapped as non-scorable identity terms.

Base material used to grow and ferment ingredients, often derived from yeast or soy. While essential for production, it's considered a passive component of the final product. Generally considered safe.

Used as a carrier oil for fat-soluble ingredients in liquid or softgel supplements. While it contains beneficial Omega-3 fatty acids, in this context, it's categorized for its passive role as a neutral base.
Blend used as a coating or glazing agent. Generally safe as a passive excipient.
Natural or artificial flavoring blend used to provide fresh fruit taste. Generally considered safe for flavoring purposes.

Derived from animal collagen, forming the shell of traditional capsules and softgels. Provides a protective casing. While animal-derived (not vegan/vegetarian), it is generally considered safe and inert in its role as a capsule material.
Emulsifier and stabilizer derived from glycerol and stearic acid. Generally recognized as safe.
A lubricant and binder derived from behenic acid. Used to prevent sticking during manufacturing and as a controlled-release agent. Generally considered safe.

Natural sweetener produced by bees from flower nectar. Used as a sweetener, binder, or flavor in supplements. Generally safe but can cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals. Not recommended for infants under 1 year.
Natural or artificial flavoring used to provide honey taste. Generally considered safe for flavoring purposes.
Plant-derived cellulose material used for vegetarian capsule shells and as a coating agent. Provides barrier protection and controlled release properties.
A semi-synthetic cellulose derivative used as a binder, thickener, and coating agent in capsules and tablets. Generally considered safe.

Glycoprotein produced by stomach parietal cells, required for vitamin B12 absorption. Added to B12 supplements for enhanced absorption, especially for those with pernicious anemia. Generally safe. Typically derived from animal (porcine) sources.
Pharmaceutical-grade polyvinylpyrrolidone used as binder, disintegrant, and solubilizer. FDA approved excipient with GRAS status. Well-established safety profile in pharmaceutical applications.
Organic acid used as pH adjuster and preservative in supplement formulations. Naturally occurring in fermented foods and muscle tissue. Generally recognized as safe.
A very common emulsifier derived from soybeans. It is considered safe, but is a major allergen and often derived from genetically modified sources unless specified otherwise.

Animal-derived organ supplement (bovine liver extract). Used as a source of iron, B vitamins, and heme protein. Not standardized to specific compounds. Generally safe when sourced from inspected animals. Risk of heavy metal accumulation in liver tissue.
Easily absorbed fatty acids derived from coconut or palm kernel oil. Used as carrier for fat-soluble vitamins and active ingredients. Generally well-tolerated.
Monounsaturated fatty acid component typically present within seed oils (e.g., coriander/parsley families). Tracked for identity mapping; not scored as a standalone therapeutic active.
Mineral pearlescent colorant/coating component used in softgel and tablet finishing.
A branded excipient ingredient. Appears to be a proprietary name for a common pharmaceutical excipient substance.
Flavoring compounds extracted from real fruits, often with carrier solvents. Generally considered safe.
A coating derived from plant sources, used to protect supplements and improve appearance. Often made from plant waxes or resins. Generally considered safe.
Generic oil listing with no specific source. Treated as a neutral carrier unless otherwise specified.
Carrier/vehicle oil identity mapping for non-therapeutic context rows.
Metabolic intermediate used in energy metabolism supplements. Emerging research on longevity benefits.

Palm-derived compound with unclear regulatory status. Not found in FDA GRAS databases as a specific ingredient. May be related to palm stearin or sodium palm kernelate but specific composition and regulatory approval uncertain.
Branded form of mannitol used as a filler, bulking agent, and sweetener in tablets. Generally recognized as safe.
Pharmaceutical grade mannitol used as bulking agent and sweetener. Well-tolerated sugar alcohol with good compaction properties for tablets.
A digestive enzyme that breaks down proteins. Generally considered safe and beneficial for digestion when used appropriately.
Descriptor-level phospholipid terms used in fatty-acid/profile disclosures. Non-specific composition; not treated as a standalone scored active ingredient.
Non-specific cannabinoid-fraction disclosure under hemp extracts. Not a uniquely identifiable active without cannabinoid profile details.
A common excipient used as a solvent, plasticizer, or lubricant. Often acts as a binder or coating agent. Generally considered safe in pharmaceutical applications.
Castor-derived solubilizer/surfactant used in encapsulation systems.
A common polymer used in tablet coatings to form protective films. Water-soluble and generally considered safe for pharmaceutical use.
Natural starch from potatoes, used as a binder and thickener. Gluten-free and generally hypoallergenic.

Resinous mixture collected by bees from tree buds and sap. Used in immune support supplements for its antimicrobial properties. Can cause contact dermatitis and allergic reactions. Classified as MODERATE severity allergen.
Protein-based coating for tablets or capsules to aid swallowing and protect ingredients.

Often used as a base or carrier for other fat-soluble ingredients, especially in softgels. While fish oil itself is an active ingredient (for Omega-3s), its role here is as a neutral, benign carrier for *other* actives.
A fundamental solvent and diluent used in liquid formulations, suspensions, and as a component in the manufacturing of capsules and tablets. Considered entirely inert and safe.
Rice-derived dextrin used as a binding agent and bulking agent. Natural carbohydrate polymer that provides structure to tablets and capsules.
A common gluten-free filler used in capsules and powders, providing bulk. Generally considered hypoallergenic and inert.
Rice-derived starch used as a binding agent, disintegrant, and bulking agent. Natural carbohydrate that aids in tablet formation and dissolution.

Glandular secretion from worker bees used to feed queen larvae. Often used as an active ingredient in supplements for its purported health benefits. WARNING: Can cause severe allergic reactions, especially in individuals with asthma or bee allergies. Classified as HIGH severity allergen.
Another common vegetable oil used as a neutral carrier in supplement formulations. Generally considered safe and inert.
Common salt used as flavor enhancer and preservative. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) but should be consumed in moderation.

A resin used as a coating, often called pharmaceutical glaze or confectioner's glaze. Derived from lac insects, so it's not vegetarian/vegan. Generally considered safe but may cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.

A very common anti-caking and flow agent in powders, capsules, and tablets, preventing ingredients from clumping. Naturally occurring mineral, generally considered safe. Merged from NHA_COLLOIDAL_SILICON_DIOXIDE on 2025-11-17.
A gum from brown algae used as a gelling agent and stabilizer. Natural, vegan-friendly, and generally considered safe. Can provide dietary fiber benefits.
Modified cellulose used as thickener and stabilizer. Generally recognized as safe.
A 'superdisintegrant' used to help tablets and capsules break apart rapidly in the digestive system for better absorption. Generally considered safe.
Nonionic emulsifier entry for normalization of sorbitan-based excipient labels and common spelling variants/typos.
Common carrier oil and excipient in softgel capsules. Provides negligible therapeutic value as a supplement ingredient. Major allergen (soy). Often from GMO sources unless specified organic.
Can be used as a stabilizer or thickener. Derived from soybeans, so may be a concern for those with soy allergies. Generally considered safe.
General stearate salt used as lubricant in tablet manufacturing. Source not specified - could be magnesium stearate, calcium stearate, or other stearate forms.
A common vegetable oil used as a neutral carrier for fat-soluble vitamins and other lipophilic compounds in supplements. Generally considered safe and inert in this role.
Natural starch from cassava root, used as a binder and filler. Gluten-free alternative to corn or wheat starch. Generally safe and hypoallergenic.
Organic acid used as acidity regulator and pH adjuster in supplement formulations. Naturally found in grapes and used in food industry. Generally recognized as safe.
A plasticizer used in tablet coatings to provide flexibility and prevent cracking. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS).
A plasticizer for tablet coatings that provides flexibility and prevents film cracking. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS).
Used as a plasticizer for coatings and as an emulsifier. Plant-derived and generally considered safe.

A plant-derived cellulose material (Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose) used to form vegetarian and vegan capsule shells. Provides a protective casing for ingredients and is generally considered inert and safe for consumption.
Plant-derived cellulose used as a bulking agent, anti-caking agent, and texture enhancer. Provides dietary fiber and is physiologically inert. Commonly used in tablet and capsule manufacturing.
General vegetable-derived fiber for bulking and digestive support.

A generic term that could refer to various plant-based gums like Gum Arabic, Xanthan Gum, or other natural gums. Specific identification recommended for accurate assessment. Generally considered safe.
A generic term for plant-derived flow agents or lubricants used in manufacturing. Often refers to ingredients like vegetable magnesium stearate. Generally considered safe.
Plant-derived fatty acid mixture used as lubricant and anti-caking agent. Alternative to animal-derived stearic acid. Generally well-tolerated.
Plant-derived fatty acid used as a lubricant and anti-caking agent in tablet manufacturing. Vegetable alternative to animal-derived stearic acid.
Common starch used as a binder or filler. Contains gluten unless specified otherwise.
An enzyme that breaks down xylan, a component of plant fiber. Generally considered safe and beneficial for digestion.
Protein hydrolysate descriptor added for mapping coverage; classify as non-scorable unless curated into IQM.
Composite mineral descriptor added for mapping coverage; requires explicit mineral breakdown for IQM scoring.
Branded multi-botanical blend name captured for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable until decomposed.
Branded blend descriptor added for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable until ingredient-level decomposition.
Generic label descriptor normalized for mapping consistency.
Acidulant used for tartness and pH control in gummies/tablets. Common food-grade additive; treated as non-scorable formulation excipient.
Acidulant/texturizer occasionally used in chewable and gummy systems. Treated as non-scorable excipient-level additive.
Ion-exchange polymer/resin used as tablet disintegrant or formulation aid. Not a therapeutic active in supplement scoring.
Generic label descriptor for hydrocolloids (e.g., pectin/agar/alginate). Treated as non-scorable excipient placeholder when specific gelling material is not disclosed.
Generic RNA label claim with unspecified source/form. Tracked as mapped informational ingredient and excluded from quality scoring until form-specific clinical mapping is curated.
Uncommon borate-salt label token observed in inactive/header context. Classified as non-scorable mapped ingredient pending regulatory/source-level curation.
Branded blend descriptor added for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable until ingredient-level decomposition.
Branded blend descriptor added for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable until ingredient-level decomposition.
Branded ingredient descriptor added for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable until ingredient-level decomposition.
Label/form-factor descriptor captured for mapping suppression; not an ingredient.
Phytochemical constituent descriptor captured for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable unless elevated to dedicated IQM entry.
Phytochemical constituent descriptor captured for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable unless elevated to dedicated IQM entry.
Phytochemical constituent descriptor captured for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable unless elevated to dedicated IQM entry.
Phytochemical constituent descriptor captured for mapping coverage; treat as non-scorable unless elevated to dedicated IQM entry.
Animal-derived oil used as a carrier in some softgel formulations; treated as non-scorable identity mapping.
Traditional glandular-derived ingredient label term used in legacy male vitality formulations; treated as non-scorable identity mapping.
Polysaccharide matrix commonly derived from fenugreek/guar and used as delivery or fiber component; mapped for identity without form-quality scoring.
Branded proprietary complex label term (often used in soy isoflavone formulas); mapped for identity to prevent unmapped leakage pending deeper manufacturer-specific composition data.
Isolated sesquiterpene lactone from Artemisia annua; mapped conservatively as identity-only pending dedicated IQM form-scoring policy for this pharmacologically active compound.
Identity mapping for castor oil label variants; role may be carrier/excipient in softgels.
Identity mapping for ethyl oleate excipient/carrier use.
Identity mapping for glyceryl monooleate emulsifier use.
